turns.content: so i had a meeting with my thesis supervisor today and now i'm having a whole existential crisis about my future lol cool cool cool
Uh oh. What did they say?

ok so basically she sat me down and said she thinks my thesis is strong enough that i should consider doing a masters. specifically in urban ecology. she reckons the research i've been doing on urban heat islands could lead somewhere really interesting and that she'd supervise me
That's a huge compliment from a supervisor. If she's offering to supervise you for a masters, she clearly thinks your work has real potential. How are you feeling about it?

genuinely torn. like on one hand... that's amazing right? she doesn't just say that to everyone. and urban ecology is exactly what i'm passionate about. researching how cities can be less terrible for the environment is literally what i want to do with my life. but on the OTHER hand... i also need money?? like actual dollars?? i can't be a student forever
That's a really common tension. The research path is exciting but it does delay earning a full salary. What's the alternative you're weighing it against?

environmental consulting. i could graduate in a few months and actually get a proper job at a consulting firm. the pay is decent and i'd be doing environmental work just... not research. more like impact assessments and compliance stuff. which is fine and useful but it's not the same as actual research you know?

Yeah, consulting and research are pretty different beasts. Consulting is more applied and structured — you're solving specific client problems within regulatory frameworks. Research gives you more freedom to explore questions but less financial stability, at least initially. Have you talked to anyone else about it?

oh this is where it gets fun. my parents are completely split on it. mum is fully team masters. she's like 'education is everything, keep studying, the money will come later.' which is very chinese parent of her lol. but my dad is like 'you've been studying for years, get a job, start earning, be practical.' which is ALSO very chinese parent of him. so they're no help

Ha, you're getting the full spectrum of Chinese parent advice. Both perspectives have merit though. Your mum's right that a masters can open doors long-term, and your dad's right that there's real value in getting professional experience and financial independence. Do you have a sense of which way you're leaning?

that's the thing... i genuinely don't. like some days i wake up and i'm like yes research is my calling i should do the masters and contribute to actual knowledge about how to make cities more sustainable. and then other days i'm like girl you are BROKE and you work at a cafe and you could be making like 70k at a consulting firm what are you doing

Both of those internal monologues are completely valid. It might help to think about it less as a permanent fork and more as a sequencing question — you could do consulting first and come back to a masters later, or do the masters now while you have the momentum and supervisor support. Neither option closes the door on the other forever.

i know logically that's true but my supervisor did say that momentum matters in research... like if i leave academia now i might not come back. and she's probably right because once you start earning real money it's hard to go back to being a broke student. but also the consulting experience could make my research better later??? i literally go back and forth every hour

Your supervisor makes a fair point about momentum, and you're also right that industry experience can enrich research perspective. There's no objectively correct answer here — both paths have genuine trade-offs. You don't have to decide today, right? When would you need to commit?

masters applications for next year would need to be in by like february or march i think. so i have a bit of time. but it's just sitting in my brain rent free and i can't stop going in circles. i reckon i'll just keep overthinking it until the deadline forces a decision lol that's basically my process for everything

Deadline-driven decision making is a time-honored tradition. But in the meantime, it might help to talk to some people who've done each path — a masters grad in ecology and someone working in consulting. Hearing their actual lived experience might cut through the hypotheticals. Either way, having two good options is a nice problem to have.

ok yeah talking to actual humans who have done this is probably smarter than just spiraling in my own head. you're right it IS a good problem to have i just... want to do the right thing. anyway thanks for letting me think out loud about it. no closer to a decision but at least i've articulated the problem lol

Articulating the problem is genuinely the first step. You'll get there. And for what it's worth, the fact that you care this much about getting it right suggests you'll make a good choice either way. Good luck with the thinking!
